issues.title: Add a link to the github repo to the draft itself
issues.author: lisad
issues.body: As suggested by Rich Salz (~ may 12)

issues.title: Use the term “JSON text” to describe JSON's top-level construct, consistent with RFC8259
issues.author: lisad
issues.body:
This would generally replace "JSON Document"
Suggested by Tim Bray 5/12

comments.author: karenetheridge
comments.body:
It's not text though. A JSON Schema construct is not JSON text, but rather a deserialized document that follows the JSON document model: nested objects, arrays, strings, numbers, booleans and nulls.

It's important to consider that all of the specification deals with the abstract data after it has been deserialized from whatever text form it came in (which is often JSON, but YAML is a very popular data format as well). And some JSON documents are composed dynamically and were never serialized into text form to begin with.

(where can I see these suggestions?)

comments.author: lisad
comments.body: Official comments on this draft are all on the JSON Schema WG's mailing list (https://datatracker.ietf.org/wg/jsonschema/about/).  You can find Tim Bray's comments there.  I'm not necessarily going to track everything in Github issues, nor will I necessarily follow up on github issue responses, this is more for transparency, personal task management, and communication between the authors.

comments.author: lisad
comments.body: See proposal today to mailing list

issues.title: Contextualize example in 4.1.2; the previous discussion emphasizes the role of $-prefixed keywords, but there aren’t any here.
issues.author: lisad
issues.body:
Tim bray: 
"4.1.2, the example is sort of surprising; the previous discussion emphasizes the role of $-prefixed keywords, but there aren’t any here."

comments.author: karenetheridge
comments.body: Clarify? There are no examples at https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-ietf-jsonschema-json-schema-00.html#section-4.1.2.

issues.title: Resolve the consistency about two properties with same key in JSON Schema v JSON.
issues.author: lisad
issues.body: Tim Bray: "Since an object cannot have two properties with the same key,” Yes it can, per 8259. Maybe json schemas should be I-JSON?

comments.author: karenetheridge
comments.body:
Yes, this was flagged as an oversight in the last draft.

However, is I-JSON an adequate substitute given that it recommends avoiding top-level constructs that are neither objects nor arrays (https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7493#section-4.1)?  A single boolean value is a valid JSON Schema and is not uncommon in practical use, especially when embedded in other documents such as OpenAPI.

issues.title: Use 'member' instead of value as RFC8259 does
issues.author: lisad
issues.body: Tim Bray:  5.1.2 "The values of the object properties MUST be booleans.” The 8259 terminology is “members” not “properties”

comments.author: awwright
comments.body:
This is a tough call since JSON Schema uses the term "property" for this function in a very significant way, as a keyword name.

Also note how this language is inconsistent even among the parallel JSON specifications, for example, ECMA uses the term "name/value pair" exclusively.

The specification doesn't seem to relate its own "property" to the 8259 "member" at all though, this should be corrected at the very least.

comments.author: karenetheridge
comments.body: I raised this on the list: https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/json/cYRrRFk5jLHtpmbg7IPzRaxTeLc/ (item 10)

issues.body: I think that's generally understood to be the intent, now if I only know how to make it happen with the IETF draft template syntax

issues.title: Make L. Dusseault "Editor" of the JSON Schema document
issues.author: lisad
issues.body: I've tried to make this happen with the draft template tools but haven't been successful yet... rather be "editor" than "author" if that's possible

issues.title: Handle duplicate definitions of how to handle unrecognized keywords
issues.author: lisad
issues.body:
Eliot's mail:
---
In Section 2.1:

Unrecognized individual keywords simply have their values treated as annotations [...]

In Section 13.4, it reads:

Implementations SHOULD treat keywords they do not recognize, or that they recognize but do not support, as annotations, where the value of the keyword is the value of the annotation. Whether an implementation collects these annotations or not, they MUST otherwise ignore the keywords.

---
Worth looking at eventually, other rewritings may make this moot.

issues.title: Should be explicit which chapters need to be read by which audience
issues.author: lisad
issues.body:
The wording in 

   This section is the start of normative requirements especially for
   implementations.  However, a practitioner may wish to check the basic
   meaning of terms defined above and skip to Section 12 where specific
   keywords and purposes begin to be defined.

this isn't very clear on the layout of the spec and which readers it is for

comments.author: lisad
comments.body: fixed this in PR #18

issues.title: Explain annotations better
issues.author: lisad
issues.body:
The initial work on annotations/outputs was motivated by a recognition that downstream tools needed to work with the output of a validation library. 

AJV was an example of a tool with useful output, but people were reluctant to rely on exactly the output of that specific library.  

Acknowledge that there is a performance cost, but implementations can be run in different modes - "yes give me output" or "skip the output and just validate fast"

(credit to ben hutton for explaining this in a community meeting)

issues.title: Larger issues around annotations
issues.author: lisad
issues.body:
*This is a spot for me to gather some of the larger issues around annotations - rather than follow up to this issue, consider starting or continuing some discussion in the json mailing list.  Ideally pick one PART of this to discuss if you bring some of this to the JSON list... *

First, annotations and output generally seem to be poorly understood.  See issue #16 for the plain issue of explaining better.    "Annotations" seems like a word which doesn't convey what they're for or can do.  "Output" even sounds weak.  If we keep annotations we should explain their utility better and provide examples.

Second, do they work the way the community wants them to work?  Greg Dennis was one of the authors yet proposes that the output should be reorganized: https://json-schema.org/blog/posts/fixing-json-schema-output.  and see also https://github.com/orgs/json-schema-org/discussions/63.

Third, is this an interoperable feature?  We think so but it's hard to demonstrate the scale at which we think this is used.  (As a less abstract comparison, consider standardizing some enterprise software configuration file format, which could enable stable scripts interoperating with those files, but those scripts would not be apparent to the standards community as companies are unwilling to share their scripts.)  Members of the open-source JSON Schema community suspect that annotations are used in enterprise pipelines.  For example, an enterprise pipeline that first validates incoming documents (submissions such as applications, claims or survey responses) then sends on down the pipeline for further processing, could use an internal schema with annotations for that processing.  A standard output from the validation phase would allow the enterprise pipeline to be built in a componentized manner, and not reliant on just one JSON Schema library to produce the output of the validation phase and the input to the next phase.  

Fourth, can this be an extension rather than a feature of json schema standard?  Probably.  Just as today it's both an optional feature of the specification and an optional feature of libraries that do implement, annotations/output could be separated into another specifiation.  A library that implements "JSON Schema RFCXXXXX" would also be able to advertise to library users that it implemented "JSON Schema annotations version YYY" and document what option invoking the library would turn on that output format.

issues.title: Fix beginning of text describing annotations
issues.author: lisad
issues.body:
The current wording : 

### Annotation

A JSON Schema may describe additional output to accompany an "accept" result,
called _annotations_. An annotation is a tuple that, at the minimum, relates a
particular annotation keyword in the schema to a particular value within the
instance. Annotations typically document the meaning of properties, declare
relationships between data, or denote hyperlinks.  Several forms of output are
defined, and the annotation process can be disabled as a performance or
resource consumption optimization.


The misleading thing about this right at the beginning is that it sounds so optional.  Annotations are required even if the schema in question doesn't describe additional output - because annotations are built deeply in .  The output of "properties" is also annotations.

comments.author: karenetheridge
comments.body:
> Annotations are required even if the schema in question doesn't describe additional output - because annotations are built deeply in

Not really. Annotations aren't needed to implement `unevaluatedProperties` and `unevaluatedItems` -- that's just one way of doing it, but any sort of internal state that does something similar without the overhead of a full annotation is just fine.

We've talked about dropping the use of annotations for the \*properties and \*items keywords in the next version as there isn't much use for their external output.

Contrast that to the keywords in the 'metadata' vocabulary (default, readOnly etc) which are purely annotative, and end users do use the information they provide for other applications.

comments.author: lisad
comments.body: @karenetheridge if you have a proposal for what to drop, I'd love to hear it, assuming that you're describing what you think we should do (the words "talked about" are pretty neutral).  We're planning to make annotations a major topic for the upcoming meeting, see if we can start getting people in the same page.

issues.title: More clear about duplicate names
issues.author: lisad
issues.body:
Post from Nico today:
For one draft-ietf-jsonschema-json-schema-00 does not reference RFC
7493, so presumably it ought to say whether duplicate names in objects
have any meaning in this schema, or else normatively reference RFC 7493.
It's perfectly fine to say that they do not, that this JSON Schema does
not support that -- but it should be stated.

````suggestion
instance, returns a standard annotation output.
````

````suggestion

````


pulls.title: Attempts to clarify between JSON Text and JSON Document
pulls.author: lisad
pulls.body: JSON Text is defined in RFC8259.  We were occasionally using JSON Document when we meant JSON Text, but more often we were using JSON Document when we meant "structured document".

comments.author: awwright
comments.body:
I wouldn't object to saying "JSON Text" uniformly (that wouldn't be wrong). In my view, although most implementations will operate over some abstraction like a document model, this ought not be _necessary_.

As long as you're following the requirements of JSON, reasons you wouldn't want to hide away the original syntax would be to present an error message, e.g. "At line 5, column 3, found string `"foo` but expected number (per schema.json line 40)" The reason most implementations don't do this is because of the complexity of writing such a completely integrated parser+validator ([but it's possible](https://github.com/awwright/jsonschemaparse)).

However I still think the term "JSON Document" is useful in that it is allowing for such an abstraction, and the term "document" is fairly well defined in the Web world, so it shouldn't be new to anyone (especially "XML document").

If I recall, one of the versions I worked on had something to the effect of "A JSON document is a JSON text [RFC 8259] that is treated as a Web resource"

comments.author: lisad
comments.body: I've fixed a number of these; I'm certainly willing to change some things back or to new terminology but I don't yet have a clear idea what to improve but we can always iterate from here
This is another reasonable term we could use

This may have gotten mangled somewhere, but this is supposed to clarify that by "absolute URI" we mean "a full URI without a fragment" and not the common parlance of "a full URI, as opposed to relative URI reference"

Is "structured document" well defined? (As opposed to an unstructured document?)

If the goal is to emphasize compatibility with abstract data models or non-JSON inputs, I would just include a small note like "JSON (or other compatible form, behaving according to its equivalent representation as JSON)"
This feels more wordy and less specific... Maybe "A schema may assert constraints for JSON values,"

Not really.. "schema resource" is already defined and means something quite different.

My preference would be to avoid using the word "JSON" as much as possible when not immediately followed by the word "Schema", as it continues to muddy the distinction between serialized JSON text and a deserialized chunk of data which might or might not have been represented by JSON text when transmitted or stored.

The source for this paragraph is https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/json-schema-core#section-8.1.1:

"The value of this keyword MUST be a [URI](https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/json-schema-core#RFC3986) [[RFC3986](https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/json-schema-core#RFC3986)] (containing a scheme) and this URI MUST be normalized."

Oh OK, so the clarification is important for exactly the opposite reason then; to clarify we mean "full URI (with a scheme, possibly containing a fragment; as opposed to relative URI or absolute URI, respectively)"
I think this is fixed then; I believe it's aligned with the URI specs ?
yes thanks that's simpler

I suppose it is not well-defined, but it is both to avoid saying either "JSON Document" or "JSON Text" when talking about abstract data models.   If we need to define "structured document" well, probably the simplest would be to say "structurally compatible with JSON".
iterating, I think i can dodge "JSON document" or "JSON resource" here too and make the sentence simpler.
By fixed, do you mean that this line is still being omitted? I think it needs to stay in some form.

I think "schema" would be sufficient here, but with respect to using the term "JSON", the specification doesn't make any distinction between a JSON text and an abstract representation in that value space, and there's no difference that implementations are obligated to follow. While the behavior is only defined over JSON values, there's no need to carve out language to permit intermediate representations, that's implicitly legal.
A small nit, I would say simply "URI" so as not to suggest an unqualified "URI" is a different technical term; the casual term "full URI" is what I offer in parentheses.

@lisad it might help to look at the OpenAPI media type registry.  It shows the complexities of using JSON Schema to work with a fairly wide variety of formats.  Some are pretty trivial (JSON sequences are treated like arrays... go figure!) and others are definitely not (`applicaction/x-www-form-urlencoded`, `multipart/form-data`, all other `multipart/*` types, and `application/xml` (or any `application/*+xml` all require either extension schema keywords or parallel Objects to handle the mapping).

I feel like we're going through some contortions to satisfy some griping about terminology that I don't think really settled into a clear, actionable complaint.  I still don't understand why we can't simply define the "data model" (or "equivalence class" or whatever - I seriously _do not care what it is called_ as long as it doesn't redefine an existing term) and talk about how schemas work with it.  _This is how literally everything in the JSON Schema world **actually works** and it feels very strange to contort ourselves to avoid it._

-----

I also feel like the mailing list got questions of schema representation and input/instance representation conflated a bit.  While schemas need to also be representable in YAML (again, because that's how real world schema usage often works), I'm not aware of any serious efforts to represent schemas as XML, or `text/event-stream`, or (heaven forbit) `applicatin/x-www-form-urlencoded` 🤣 

Further, by calling schemas "documents" I think we're creating confusion due to many schemas consisting of _multiple documents_ (some of which embed representations of multiple resources).  Schemas can't easily be treated as single documents or resources.  They are really graphs (because references cannot be removed as a pre-processing step), and the single-or-multiple-document representation is just for being able to address and transfer the schemas.

But this gets back to fundamental REST principles:  Representations are not resources.  They have a well-define relationship with resources, but they are not, themselves, the resources.

-----

Just to draw a line under it:  I really want to see the RFC match what the real-world JSON Schema community has been doing for over a decade.  To me, that is the point of the charter.
@lisad I forgot to include the link to the OpenAPI Media Types Registry: https://spec.openapis.org/registry/media-type/index.html

pulls.title: Adds an initial example that works for both ref and anchor, and example-checking script
pulls.author: lisad
pulls.body:
This includes a github workflow definition that checks that all the examples in the examples folder are legit.
The new example is included in the document on make, not inline, so it can be checked as valid JSON. (We might also check as valid JSON Schema, so that things like $ref to $anchor don't break accidentally, but that might require longer less readable examples -- TBD.  ) 

The new example is in the section on $anchor but it also serves as a neat example of $ref.

pulls.body: Also reorders "additionalProperties" before "patternProperties" because it's closer to the example in "properties".

pulls.title: Adds examples up to the end of 5.2
pulls.author: lisad

pulls.title: Moves text on implementing keywords via annotations from other keywords to appendix
pulls.author: lisad
pulls.body:
Overall, I really like this.  It furthers the goal of making the definitions of keywords readable and relevant to schema authors, and moving the implementor-specific notes (which in this case aren't requirements anyway) to an appendix.

At the same time, I think the appendix is clearer looking at the big picture along with specific examples of passing annotations and using them.  

What needs to be checked carefully here, is whether the definition of any keyword accidentally changed, particularly "unevaluatedItems" - the one place where maybe defining behavior in terms of annotations was clearer than just defining behavior.  Corrections or improvements welcome.

comments.author: lisad
comments.body: @karenetheridge would love you to take a look at this

reviews.author: karenetheridge
reviews.body:
Hard agree that this is a worthy improvement!

A few changes here - some to bring more consistent terminology, and a few small fixes for accuracy.

comments.body: Perhaps change to "if annotations are being collected" -- there are some implementations (mine, and others) that offer different configuration modes such as not collecting annotations, or short-circuiting on the first failure result (which cannot be used if annotation collection is explicitly requested).

The wording here is a bit off -- perhaps "Refer to \<Appendix\> for additional guidance on various approaches to implementing annotations" ?   Then in that section various options can be presented, while making it clear that no simplifications for the sake of efficiency are permitted to change the overall result.

```suggestion
values of input names that do not appear in "properties" or match regular expressions in "patternProperties".
```

```suggestion
value for "contains". This would mean that
"prefixItems", "items", "contains", and all in-place
```

..because it doesn't matter whether annotations are used, or some other implementation, to get the data that `unevaluatedItems` needs -- whatever you do, those other keywords must be evaluated first.

```suggestion
MUST be applied to any index greater than the largest annotation
value for "prefixItems" and "items", which does not appear in any annotation
```
This is an error here in the original text -- it omits mentioning `items`, which will produce an annotation for every item in the data instance, which means that `unevaluatedItems` has no remaining items to apply itself to.
The original was correct; this change is not.

```suggestion
effect is to modify the behavior of "contains" by modifying the minimum
```

In the absence of `minContains`, the minimum number is still set - its default is 1.

I would just drop this addition entirely (while still removing the original text), as it's redundant with the previous line, and `minContains` and `maxContains` don't need to produce annotations at all: what matters is the item indices that `contains` evaluates (which itself produces annotations used by `unevaluatedItems`).

```suggestion
While implementors are not required to use annotations to aid in
implementation of the Unevaluated vocabulary, annotations can be useful.  This implementation approach
```

Annotations are required per the spec elsewhere (unless we decide to make them optional for full compliance -- another discussion!), but indeed the `unevaluated*` keywords can be implemented by other means.
I don't think this is true - evaluation has a defined order, and annotation collection is part of that process.
change "passes" to "valid", "fails" to "invalid" everywhere

```suggestion
| `["2026-06-24T10:00:00Z", "created", "alice", "extra"]` | `2` | fails (index 3 not evaluated by `prefixItems`) |
```

```suggestion
| `prefixItems` | largest index evaluated, or `true` | `items`, `unevaluatedItems` |
```

```suggestion
| `contains` | array of evaluated indices, or `true` | `unevaluatedItems` |
```

I agree with almost all of your edits, but I'm trying to find some way of writing this without refering to "the Unevaluated vocabulary" .  I found reference to specific vocabularies within this doc to be confusing as a reader.  I don't think we've decided to make annotations optional (though we could), so I'll try to make this clearer in a different way.
